title: Disc Herniation
oneLiner: A tear/protrusion of intervertebral disc material causing nerve root compression; common in young-to-middle-aged adults; missed cauda equina is a medicolegal minefield.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 35-year-old male builder presents with sudden-onset severe low back pain after lifting a heavy concrete block. The back pain resolved over two days, but he now has a sharp, burning pain radiating down the right posterior thigh and calf into the lateral foot. He says “I can’t sit still in the chair” – sitting worsens the leg pain, and leaning forward to tie his shoes is excruciating. On exam, he stands with a list to the left (antalgic scoliosis), straight leg raise (SLR) on the right reproduces leg pain at 40 degrees, and the right ankle jerk is absent.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Pattern Clue | What It Tells You |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Young male, heavy lifting (occupational) | Most typical risk factor for acute disc herniation |
| Back pain *then* leg pain (sequential) | Disc initially bulges (back pain from annular stretch), then ruptures — nucleus compresses nerve root (leg pain) |
| Burning, sharp leg pain along a dermatome (posterior thigh-calf-lateral foot) | Radicular pain = nerve root irritation (here S1 nerve root) |
| Sitting worsens leg pain | Sitting increases intradiscal pressure → more nerve compression |
| Antalgic scoliosis (lean away from pain) | Muscle spasm to splint the irritated nerve root — classic, not a “fake” spine |
| Positive SLR at 40° (reproduces leg pain) | Mechanical stretch of inflamed nerve root; <45° is very suggestive |
| Absent ankle jerk (S1) | Motor/sensory loss at S1 = disc compressing S1 nerve root (L5-S1 disc) |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **Cauda equina syndrome (CES) mimic** – Bilateral leg pain, saddle anaesthesia, urinary retention. The junior often dismisses “just a disc” but this is a surgical emergency. Clue: ask about “have you wet yourself?” – incontinence is a red flag.
2. **Cervical disc herniation** – Pain in the neck radiating to the scapula or down the arm, with a C6 or C7 dermatomal pattern. Exam may show a “winged scapula” or weak biceps/triceps. Often missed as a shoulder strain.
3. **Lateral (far lateral) disc herniation** – Pain opposite to the expected side. SLR may be negative. The clue is radicular pain that persists despite negative SLR / crossed SLR.
4. **Central disc herniation (bilateral radiculopathy)** – Bilateral pain, sometimes with early motor loss but no clear dermatomal pattern. Watch for subtle bladder symptoms.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
A disc herniation occurs when the tough outer layer (annulus fibrosus) of an intervertebral disc tears, allowing the soft inner gel (nucleus pulposus) to bulge out or rupture. This material compresses or inflames a nearby spinal nerve root, causing pain, numbness, or weakness along the nerve’s distribution — most commonly in the leg (sciatica) or arm (cervical radiculopathy).

**Why this topic matters clinically:**  
- **Frequency:** Lumbar disc herniation is one of the most common causes of low back pain and sciatica in adults under 50. An estimated 1–3% of the population will have a symptomatic disc herniation in their lifetime.  
- **Stakes:** While most herniations resolve with conservative care, a missed cauda equina syndrome leads to permanent paralysis, incontinence, and major litigation.  
- **Common pitfalls:**  
  - Mistaking radicular pain for “hip pain” or “gluteal strain” (missed diagnosis).  
  - Not documenting motor/sensory exams in the acute setting (medicolegal risk).  
  - Over-investigating with plain X-rays (poor sensitivity; MRI is gold standard).  
  - Under-recognising cervical disc herniations — the patient gets sent to physio with “neck strain” but has an evolving myelopathy.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story of how it develops:**  
The intervertebral disc is a shock absorber. With age, the nucleus pulposus becomes less hydrated and more fibrous, and the annulus fibrosus develops tiny radial tears (degenerative change). A sudden or repeated axial load (e.g., lifting with a flexed spine, jumping off a ladder) increases intradiscal pressure beyond the strength of the weakened annulus → the tear extends, and nucleus material is forced outward. This is a “contained” bulging (protrusion) if the outer annulus is intact; if the annulus is completely breached, it’s a true herniation (extrusion or even sequestration where a fragment detaches).

**Why each key clinical sign occurs (mechanism → sign):**

| Clinical Sign | Pathophysiological Mechanism | Reason |
|---------------|------------------------------|--------|
| Low back pain (first symptom) | Annular tear releases inflammatory mediators (prostaglandins, substance P) → irritation of sinuvertebral nerve (a recurrent meningeal nerve) | Back pain often precedes leg pain by hours to days — the “disc pain” phase |
| Radicular leg pain (sciatica) | Extruded nucleus compresses dorsal root ganglion or nerve root; also chemical irritation (phospholipase A2) | Pain follows dermatome (L4, L5, S1 most common) |
| Positive straight leg raise (SLR) | SLR stretches the sciatic nerve; if nerve root is already inflamed/compressed, stretch increases tension → pain | SLR <45° reproduces pain; crossed SLR (pain in the affected leg when raising the *contralateral* leg) is highly specific for disc herniation |
| Sensory loss / paraesthesia | Compression of afferent fibers in nerve root | Dermatomal numbness/tingling; S1 → lateral foot; L5 → dorsum of foot; L4 → medial shin |
| Motor weakness | Compression of efferent fibers; reduced conduction velocity or axonal injury | L5 → foot drop (weak tibialis anterior); S1 → weak plantarflexion (toe walking); L4 → quadriceps weakness |
| Reflex loss (ankle jerk absent) | S1 nerve root compression impairs the monosynaptic reflex arc | Ankle jerk ↔ S1; knee jerk ↔ L4 |
| Cauda equina syndrome (urinary retention, saddle anaesthesia) | Large central disc compresses multiple nerve roots of the cauda equina (below L1–L2); parasympathetic fibers (S2–S4) control bladder and sphincter | Bilateral symptoms, perianal numbness, loss of anal tone |

**Connecting pathophysiology to pattern recognition:**  
The classic vignette’s “absent ankle jerk” is not a nonspecific finding — it’s a test of the S1 reflex arc. The antalgic lean is because leaning *away* from the affected side reduces pressure on the compressed nerve as it exits the foramen. The sitting-worsening is because sitting increases intradiscal pressure by ~40% compared to lying; leaning forward in a chair adds more.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Degenerative (most common)** | Age-related disc desiccation + annular tears (often spontaneous) | “Gradual onset back pain, now suddenly worse after bending” - no single trauma |
| **Mechanical (acute)** | Heavy lifting with flexed spine / twisting | “Lifting a 50 kg bag – felt a ‘pop’ in the back” |
| **Occupational** | Prolonged sitting (driving, desk) + lifting; jobs with vibration (truck drivers) | “I drive for 10 hours a day; have had backaches for years” |
| **Traumatic** | Fall from height, RTA (axial loading + flexion) | “Fell 3 metres onto his feet, now leg pain” |
| **Genetic / structural** | Ehlers-Danlos, Marfan; rare congenital spinal stenosis can predispose | “Multiple family members with herniated discs in their 30s” |
| **Iatrogenic** | Post-laminectomy or post-discectomy at other levels (accelerated degeneration) | “After L4-L5 discectomy 2 years ago, now has new L5-S1 pain” |
| **Smoking (modifiable risk factor)** | Nicotine reduces disc blood supply → accelerates degeneration | “Smoker of 20 pack-years with chronic back pain” |

**Most common cause:** Degenerative changes + repetitive microtrauma (often no single inciting event). Acute on chronic.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
No absolute scoring system; clinical diagnosis is primary. The “Canadian C‑Spine Rule” for cervical imaging and “CLBP guidelines” exist but are not exclusive for disc. For lumbar radiculopathy, **clinical criteria** suffice: dermatomal leg pain + corresponding reflex or motor loss + positive SLR. **Urgent MRI** is indicated only for cauda equina syndrome, progressive motor deficit, or failure of conservative therapy after 4–6 weeks.

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| **MRI (gold standard)** | High-resolution images: disc contour, nerve root compression, inflammation (T2 high signal) | Confirm location and severity; rule out abscess/tumour; plan surgery | Single finding that confirms: **nerve root compression by disc material** at the corresponding level |
| **Plain X-ray (AP + lateral)** | Disc height loss, osteophytes, instability (flexion-extension views) | Rule out fracture, spondylolisthesis, or malignancy; no soft tissue detail | Normal X-ray does NOT exclude herniation — false sense of security |
| **CT myelogram** (if MRI contraindicated) | Contrast outlines thecal sac; compressive lesions appear as filling defects | MRI unsafe (pacemaker, claustrophobia) or non-diagnostic | Filling defect at disc level = herniation |
| **EMG/NCS** | Delayed conduction in affected nerve root; denervation potentials | Chronic radiculopathy, unclear diagnosis, or to differentiate from peripheral neuropathy | Positive waves / fibrillations in L5 or S1 myotome = root damage |
| **Lumbar puncture** (rare) | High protein, no cells (if herniation alone); but not first line | Suspect infection or inflammatory radiculitis | Not helpful for routine disc → avoid unless symptoms suggest meningitis |

**Pattern-recognition for single finding that confirms:**  
MRI showing a **posterolateral disc herniation at L4-L5 or L5-S1 with displacement of the traversing nerve root** (e.g., S1 root at L5-S1 disc). In a patient with classic sciatica, that MRI is diagnostic.

**What rules out:**  
A completely normal MRI (no nerve root compression) suggests alternative diagnosis (e.g., lumbar strain, hip pathology, peripheral nerve entrapment). However, small bulges can be incidental in asymptomatic adults — correlate clinically.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour in ED / clinic):**

| Step | What | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| **History + neuro exam** | Check for red flags: bilateral leg pain, saddle anaesthesia, urinary retention, progressive weakness, fever | Identify cauda equina / infection |
| **Analgesia** | NSAID (ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO q6h PRN) + paracetamol 1 g PO q6h | Reduce inflammation at nerve root |
| **Avoid prolonged bed rest** | Encourage early mobilisation (avoid >48h bed rest) | Bed rest worsens debility, slows recovery |
| **Urgent MRI + neurosurgery consult** if cauda equina or severe motor deficit (< 3/5) | Immediate referral | CES must be operated within 24–48h |

**Ward (24–72 hours) / Outpatient phase (if no red flags):**

| Step | What | Why | Dose/Duration |
|------|------|-----|---------------|
| **Neuropathic pain** | Gabapentin | Reduce neuropathic component of radicular pain | Start 300 mg PO once daily (evening); titrate to 300 mg TID over 3 weeks; max 3600 mg/day (up to 1200 mg TID) |
| **Muscle relaxant** | Diazepam 5 mg PO PRN (short-term) | For painful muscle spasm; use only 3–5 days due to tolerance/dependence | 5 mg PO at night or TID |
| **Corticosteroids** | **Do NOT use systemic corticosteroids** for simple radiculopathy (no evidence) | Can cause side effects with no proven benefit | N/A |
| **Epidural steroid injection** | Consider after 4–6 weeks of failed conservative therapy | Anti-inflammatory at nerve root; delays need for surgery | Typically under fluoroscopic guidance; single or multiple |
| **Physiotherapy** | Core strengthening, hamstring stretches, McKenzie extension exercises | Reduce recurrence, improve function | Refer for 6–8 weeks |
| **Surgical consult** | If progressive motor loss, or > 6 weeks severe pain despite conservative care | Discectomy (microdiscectomy) has better short-term outcomes | Urgency depends on deficit |

**Long-term management:**

| Step | What | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| **Lifestyle modifications** | Weight loss, smoking cessation, ergonomic lifting | Reduce recurrence risk |
| **Core stability** | Pilates / yoga / supervised physio | Strengthen paraspinal muscles, reduce disc load |
| **Recurrence prevention** | Teach patient to avoid heavy lifting with straight legs; use knees | Re-herniation rate 5–15% after conservative treatment |

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**

- **Do NOT** give systemic corticosteroids (IV methylprednisolone) — no proven benefit for disc radiculopathy and increased infection risk.
- **Do NOT** order a full spine MRI for simple back pain without radicular signs — low yield and wastes resource.
- **Do NOT** prescribe opioids as first-line for chronic radicular pain — risk of dependence.
- **Do NOT** tell patient “it will go away in 2 weeks” — symptoms may persist 6–12 weeks; set realistic expectations.
- **Do NOT** perform a lumbar puncture in a patient with suspected disc herniation — risk of herniation worsening if LP is at the wrong level.
- **Do NOT** immobilise with a brace — no evidence; early mobilisation is better.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Disc Herniation | Lumbar Strain (mechanical back pain) | Lumbar Spinal Stenosis | Hip Osteoarthritis (referred pain) | Sacroiliac Joint Dysfunction |
|---------|----------------|--------------------------------------|------------------------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Pain distribution** | Radicular (dermatomal) leg pain; back pain often resolves | Non-radicular, dull central low back, no leg pain | Bilateral leg pain/aching with walking (pseudoclaudication), relieved by bending forward | Groin pain ± referred to knee; lateral thigh rare | Unilateral gluteal pain; can radiate to posterior thigh but not below knee |
| **Exacerbating factors** | Sitting, coughing, sneezing, lifting (increase intradiscal pressure) | Movement aggravates; but no specific root stretch | Walking or standing (extends spine → narrows canal) | Weight-bearing, internal rotation of hip | Standing on one leg, climbing stairs, unilateral loading |
| **Physical exam** | +SLR, +crossed SLR, reflex loss, dermatomal sensory loss, motor weakness | Normal SLR (may have mild low back pain but no leg pain); no reflex loss | Normal SLR; neurologic exam often normal at rest; may have broad-based gait | Hip flexion, internal rotation, abduction limited (FABER test +) | FABER test + (pain in SI region); >2/4 SI provocation tests positive |
| **Imaging** | MRI: disc protrusion with nerve compression | Normal MRI or minor degenerative changes | MRI: central canal stenosis (sagittal diameter <10 mm) | X-ray hip: joint space narrowing, osteophytes | X-ray SI joints normal; MRI STIR shows marrow edema (sacroliitis) |
| **Key differentiating feature** | **Dermatomal leg pain + corresponding reflex loss + positive SLR** | No radicular symptoms; negative SLR; resolves with simple analgesia | **Bilateral leg pain with walking improves on sitting/bending forward** | **Groin pain + limited hip internal rotation** | **Unilateral gluteal pain + SI provocation tests; no dermatomal pattern** |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**1. Cauda Equina Syndrome (CES)** – The must-not-miss complication.

- **How it presents:** Bilateral leg pain or numbness, saddle anaesthesia (perineum, buttocks, inner thighs), urinary retention (overflow incontinence), faecal incontinence, loss of anal tone, bilateral lower limb weakness (often flaccid).
- **Key signs:**  
  - Decreased or absent perianal sensation (S2–S4).  
  - Loss of anal wink reflex.  
  - Post-void residual volume >200 mL on bladder scan.  
  - GCS normal (unless spinal shock).
- **What to do:**  
  - **Immediate:** Urgent MRI (whole spine; may be from a higher level).  
  - **Concurrent:** Neurosurgery consult — do not wait for MRI results if clinical suspicion is high.  
  - **Timing:** Surgery within 24 hours of symptom onset significantly improves outcomes.  
  - **Pitfall:** Delayed diagnosis due to “the patient says they can still pass urine” (but may be in overflow incontinence). Always perform a bladder scan.

**2. Chronic radiculopathy / chronic pain:**

- **Presentation:** Persistent numbness, paraesthesia, or weakness beyond 3 months despite conservative care.  
- **Key signs:** Muscle atrophy (quadriceps L4, foot dorsiflexors L5), foot drop (L5), chronic ankle weakness.  
- **What to do:** EMG/NCS to quantify denervation; consider surgical decompression if significant weakness or pain.

**3. Progressive neurological deficit:**

- **How it presents:** Worsening motor loss (e.g., from slight foot drop to complete loss of dorsiflexion).  
- **Key signs:** Serial MRC scores dropping from 4+ to 3/5 or less over days/weeks.  
- **What to do:** Urgent surgical referral — motor loss may become irreversible after 6 months.

**4. Epidural abscess / discitis (rare but serious):**

- **How it presents:** Severe back pain with fever, night sweats, elevated CRP/ESR, leucocytosis; new or worsening neurological signs; may be post-procedural (e.g., after epidural injection).  
- **Key signs:** Fever, spinal tenderness on percussion, rigid paraspinal muscles.  
- **Imaging:** MRI with contrast shows high signal within disc space or epidural collection.  
- **Management:** Urgent microbiology consult; IV antibiotics (e.g., flucloxacillin 2 g q4h + rifampicin) and possible surgical drainage.

**Late-presentation patterns (the “untreated” stem):**

- A 60-year-old presents with bilateral foot drop and chronic urinary incontinence of 3 months’ duration, with MRI showing a large central disc herniation at L4-L5. This suggests missed CES — now likely permanent. She will need long-term catheterisation, mobility aids, and a multidisciplinary rehab team. Medicolegally, the delay is indefensible.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **Cauda Equina Syndrome: “Bowel, Bladder, Saddle, Motor, Shock”**  
   - **B**owel: loss of rectal tone / incontinence  
   - **B**ladder: retention / overflow incontinence  
   - **S**addle: perineal sensory loss (saddle anaesthesia)  
   - **M**otor: bilateral lower limb weakness (flaccid)  
   - **S**hock: spinal shock (hypotension, bradycardia) — rare but dramatic

2. **For lumbar nerve root levels (L4, L5, S1): “LPS” (Lay Patient Slow)**  
   - **L4**: Lateral thigh, knee jerk ↓, quadriceps weakness  
   - **P** (L5): Posterolateral shin/dorsum foot, no reflex, foot drop  
   - **S** (S1): Posterior calf/lateral foot, ankle jerk ↓, plantarflexion weakness

3. **Red flags for back pain (disc-specific): “MICTURITION”**  
   - **M**otor weakness (progressive)  
   - **I**ntractable pain (uncontrolled)  
   - **C**auda equina (bladder/bowel)  
   - **T**rauma (fracture risk)  
   - **U**rine retention  
   - **R**eflex loss (unilateral)  
   - **I**nfection (fever)  
   - **T**umour (night pain, weight loss)  
   - **I**mmunosuppression  
   - **O**ld age (>70)  
   - **N**eurological deficit (progressive)

**High-yield clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Crossed SLR = high specificity (90%) for disc herniation.** If raising the unaffected leg reproduces pain in the affected leg, you’re almost always dealing with a nerve root compression.
2. **Cough/sneeze test:** Patient says “when I cough, I go through the roof” — typical of disc herniation. The Valsalva increases intraspinal pressure, compressing the nerve root further.
3. **Sitting forward asymmetry:** Patient leans to one side in the waiting chair — that is an antalgic lean (opposite to the side of compression in lumbar disc). This is not “bad posture” — it’s a diagnostic clue.
4. **Foot drop + positive SLR always think L5 disc herniation** (L4-L5 disc). If a patient presents with foot drop, do a lumbar neuro exam — don’t jump to stroke or peroneal neuropathy without ruling out disc.
5. **Bilateral leg pain without back pain = high suspicion for central disc or spinal stenosis.** Don’t assume it’s a simple strain.
6. **Recurrent disc herniation after surgery (same level)** presents with identical symptoms after a symptom-free period of weeks to months. Suspect re-herniation; MRI with gadolinium may help differentiate scar vs disc (scar enhances).
7. **Cervical radiculopathy: Spurling’s test** — extend and rotate the neck to the symptomatic side and apply axial compression. Reproduction of arm pain is positive.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **Not doing a perianal sensory exam or rectal tone in anyone with bilateral leg symptoms** – This is the single biggest cause of missed CES.
- **Assuming a normal MRI of the lumbar spine rules out disc herniation** – Tiny lateral discs may be missed; correlate clinically.
- **Giving a muscle relaxant for “back spasm” without checking reflexes** – You may miss a S1 radiculopathy that will later show ankle weakness.
- **Ordering a plain X-ray

rawMarkdown:
## Disc Herniation

**One-liner:** A tear/protrusion of intervertebral disc material causing nerve root compression; common in young-to-middle-aged adults; missed cauda equina is a medicolegal minefield.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 35-year-old male builder presents with sudden-onset severe low back pain after lifting a heavy concrete block. The back pain resolved over two days, but he now has a sharp, burning pain radiating down the right posterior thigh and calf into the lateral foot. He says “I can’t sit still in the chair” – sitting worsens the leg pain, and leaning forward to tie his shoes is excruciating. On exam, he stands with a list to the left (antalgic scoliosis), straight leg raise (SLR) on the right reproduces leg pain at 40 degrees, and the right ankle jerk is absent.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Pattern Clue | What It Tells You |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Young male, heavy lifting (occupational) | Most typical risk factor for acute disc herniation |
| Back pain *then* leg pain (sequential) | Disc initially bulges (back pain from annular stretch), then ruptures — nucleus compresses nerve root (leg pain) |
| Burning, sharp leg pain along a dermatome (posterior thigh-calf-lateral foot) | Radicular pain = nerve root irritation (here S1 nerve root) |
| Sitting worsens leg pain | Sitting increases intradiscal pressure → more nerve compression |
| Antalgic scoliosis (lean away from pain) | Muscle spasm to splint the irritated nerve root — classic, not a “fake” spine |
| Positive SLR at 40° (reproduces leg pain) | Mechanical stretch of inflamed nerve root; <45° is very suggestive |
| Absent ankle jerk (S1) | Motor/sensory loss at S1 = disc compressing S1 nerve root (L5-S1 disc) |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **Cauda equina syndrome (CES) mimic** – Bilateral leg pain, saddle anaesthesia, urinary retention. The junior often dismisses “just a disc” but this is a surgical emergency. Clue: ask about “have you wet yourself?” – incontinence is a red flag.
2. **Cervical disc herniation** – Pain in the neck radiating to the scapula or down the arm, with a C6 or C7 dermatomal pattern. Exam may show a “winged scapula” or weak biceps/triceps. Often missed as a shoulder strain.
3. **Lateral (far lateral) disc herniation** – Pain opposite to the expected side. SLR may be negative. The clue is radicular pain that persists despite negative SLR / crossed SLR.
4. **Central disc herniation (bilateral radiculopathy)** – Bilateral pain, sometimes with early motor loss but no clear dermatomal pattern. Watch for subtle bladder symptoms.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
A disc herniation occurs when the tough outer layer (annulus fibrosus) of an intervertebral disc tears, allowing the soft inner gel (nucleus pulposus) to bulge out or rupture. This material compresses or inflames a nearby spinal nerve root, causing pain, numbness, or weakness along the nerve’s distribution — most commonly in the leg (sciatica) or arm (cervical radiculopathy).

**Why this topic matters clinically:**  
- **Frequency:** Lumbar disc herniation is one of the most common causes of low back pain and sciatica in adults under 50. An estimated 1–3% of the population will have a symptomatic disc herniation in their lifetime.  
- **Stakes:** While most herniations resolve with conservative care, a missed cauda equina syndrome leads to permanent paralysis, incontinence, and major litigation.  
- **Common pitfalls:**  
  - Mistaking radicular pain for “hip pain” or “gluteal strain” (missed diagnosis).  
  - Not documenting motor/sensory exams in the acute setting (medicolegal risk).  
  - Over-investigating with plain X-rays (poor sensitivity; MRI is gold standard).  
  - Under-recognising cervical disc herniations — the patient gets sent to physio with “neck strain” but has an evolving myelopathy.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story of how it develops:**  
The intervertebral disc is a shock absorber. With age, the nucleus pulposus becomes less hydrated and more fibrous, and the annulus fibrosus develops tiny radial tears (degenerative change). A sudden or repeated axial load (e.g., lifting with a flexed spine, jumping off a ladder) increases intradiscal pressure beyond the strength of the weakened annulus → the tear extends, and nucleus material is forced outward. This is a “contained” bulging (protrusion) if the outer annulus is intact; if the annulus is completely breached, it’s a true herniation (extrusion or even sequestration where a fragment detaches).

**Why each key clinical sign occurs (mechanism → sign):**

| Clinical Sign | Pathophysiological Mechanism | Reason |
|---------------|------------------------------|--------|
| Low back pain (first symptom) | Annular tear releases inflammatory mediators (prostaglandins, substance P) → irritation of sinuvertebral nerve (a recurrent meningeal nerve) | Back pain often precedes leg pain by hours to days — the “disc pain” phase |
| Radicular leg pain (sciatica) | Extruded nucleus compresses dorsal root ganglion or nerve root; also chemical irritation (phospholipase A2) | Pain follows dermatome (L4, L5, S1 most common) |
| Positive straight leg raise (SLR) | SLR stretches the sciatic nerve; if nerve root is already inflamed/compressed, stretch increases tension → pain | SLR <45° reproduces pain; crossed SLR (pain in the affected leg when raising the *contralateral* leg) is highly specific for disc herniation |
| Sensory loss / paraesthesia | Compression of afferent fibers in nerve root | Dermatomal numbness/tingling; S1 → lateral foot; L5 → dorsum of foot; L4 → medial shin |
| Motor weakness | Compression of efferent fibers; reduced conduction velocity or axonal injury | L5 → foot drop (weak tibialis anterior); S1 → weak plantarflexion (toe walking); L4 → quadriceps weakness |
| Reflex loss (ankle jerk absent) | S1 nerve root compression impairs the monosynaptic reflex arc | Ankle jerk ↔ S1; knee jerk ↔ L4 |
| Cauda equina syndrome (urinary retention, saddle anaesthesia) | Large central disc compresses multiple nerve roots of the cauda equina (below L1–L2); parasympathetic fibers (S2–S4) control bladder and sphincter | Bilateral symptoms, perianal numbness, loss of anal tone |

**Connecting pathophysiology to pattern recognition:**  
The classic vignette’s “absent ankle jerk” is not a nonspecific finding — it’s a test of the S1 reflex arc. The antalgic lean is because leaning *away* from the affected side reduces pressure on the compressed nerve as it exits the foramen. The sitting-worsening is because sitting increases intradiscal pressure by ~40% compared to lying; leaning forward in a chair adds more.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Degenerative (most common)** | Age-related disc desiccation + annular tears (often spontaneous) | “Gradual onset back pain, now suddenly worse after bending” - no single trauma |
| **Mechanical (acute)** | Heavy lifting with flexed spine / twisting | “Lifting a 50 kg bag – felt a ‘pop’ in the back” |
| **Occupational** | Prolonged sitting (driving, desk) + lifting; jobs with vibration (truck drivers) | “I drive for 10 hours a day; have had backaches for years” |
| **Traumatic** | Fall from height, RTA (axial loading + flexion) | “Fell 3 metres onto his feet, now leg pain” |
| **Genetic / structural** | Ehlers-Danlos, Marfan; rare congenital spinal stenosis can predispose | “Multiple family members with herniated discs in their 30s” |
| **Iatrogenic** | Post-laminectomy or post-discectomy at other levels (accelerated degeneration) | “After L4-L5 discectomy 2 years ago, now has new L5-S1 pain” |
| **Smoking (modifiable risk factor)** | Nicotine reduces disc blood supply → accelerates degeneration | “Smoker of 20 pack-years with chronic back pain” |

**Most common cause:** Degenerative changes + repetitive microtrauma (often no single inciting event). Acute on chronic.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
No absolute scoring system; clinical diagnosis is primary. The “Canadian C‑Spine Rule” for cervical imaging and “CLBP guidelines” exist but are not exclusive for disc. For lumbar radiculopathy, **clinical criteria** suffice: dermatomal leg pain + corresponding reflex or motor loss + positive SLR. **Urgent MRI** is indicated only for cauda equina syndrome, progressive motor deficit, or failure of conservative therapy after 4–6 weeks.

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| **MRI (gold standard)** | High-resolution images: disc contour, nerve root compression, inflammation (T2 high signal) | Confirm location and severity; rule out abscess/tumour; plan surgery | Single finding that confirms: **nerve root compression by disc material** at the corresponding level |
| **Plain X-ray (AP + lateral)** | Disc height loss, osteophytes, instability (flexion-extension views) | Rule out fracture, spondylolisthesis, or malignancy; no soft tissue detail | Normal X-ray does NOT exclude herniation — false sense of security |
| **CT myelogram** (if MRI contraindicated) | Contrast outlines thecal sac; compressive lesions appear as filling defects | MRI unsafe (pacemaker, claustrophobia) or non-diagnostic | Filling defect at disc level = herniation |
| **EMG/NCS** | Delayed conduction in affected nerve root; denervation potentials | Chronic radiculopathy, unclear diagnosis, or to differentiate from peripheral neuropathy | Positive waves / fibrillations in L5 or S1 myotome = root damage |
| **Lumbar puncture** (rare) | High protein, no cells (if herniation alone); but not first line | Suspect infection or inflammatory radiculitis | Not helpful for routine disc → avoid unless symptoms suggest meningitis |

**Pattern-recognition for single finding that confirms:**  
MRI showing a **posterolateral disc herniation at L4-L5 or L5-S1 with displacement of the traversing nerve root** (e.g., S1 root at L5-S1 disc). In a patient with classic sciatica, that MRI is diagnostic.

**What rules out:**  
A completely normal MRI (no nerve root compression) suggests alternative diagnosis (e.g., lumbar strain, hip pathology, peripheral nerve entrapment). However, small bulges can be incidental in asymptomatic adults — correlate clinically.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour in ED / clinic):**

| Step | What | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| **History + neuro exam** | Check for red flags: bilateral leg pain, saddle anaesthesia, urinary retention, progressive weakness, fever | Identify cauda equina / infection |
| **Analgesia** | NSAID (ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO q6h PRN) + paracetamol 1 g PO q6h | Reduce inflammation at nerve root |
| **Avoid prolonged bed rest** | Encourage early mobilisation (avoid >48h bed rest) | Bed rest worsens debility, slows recovery |
| **Urgent MRI + neurosurgery consult** if cauda equina or severe motor deficit (< 3/5) | Immediate referral | CES must be operated within 24–48h |

**Ward (24–72 hours) / Outpatient phase (if no red flags):**

| Step | What | Why | Dose/Duration |
|------|------|-----|---------------|
| **Neuropathic pain** | Gabapentin | Reduce neuropathic component of radicular pain | Start 300 mg PO once daily (evening); titrate to 300 mg TID over 3 weeks; max 3600 mg/day (up to 1200 mg TID) |
| **Muscle relaxant** | Diazepam 5 mg PO PRN (short-term) | For painful muscle spasm; use only 3–5 days due to tolerance/dependence | 5 mg PO at night or TID |
| **Corticosteroids** | **Do NOT use systemic corticosteroids** for simple radiculopathy (no evidence) | Can cause side effects with no proven benefit | N/A |
| **Epidural steroid injection** | Consider after 4–6 weeks of failed conservative therapy | Anti-inflammatory at nerve root; delays need for surgery | Typically under fluoroscopic guidance; single or multiple |
| **Physiotherapy** | Core strengthening, hamstring stretches, McKenzie extension exercises | Reduce recurrence, improve function | Refer for 6–8 weeks |
| **Surgical consult** | If progressive motor loss, or > 6 weeks severe pain despite conservative care | Discectomy (microdiscectomy) has better short-term outcomes | Urgency depends on deficit |

**Long-term management:**

| Step | What | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| **Lifestyle modifications** | Weight loss, smoking cessation, ergonomic lifting | Reduce recurrence risk |
| **Core stability** | Pilates / yoga / supervised physio | Strengthen paraspinal muscles, reduce disc load |
| **Recurrence prevention** | Teach patient to avoid heavy lifting with straight legs; use knees | Re-herniation rate 5–15% after conservative treatment |

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**

- **Do NOT** give systemic corticosteroids (IV methylprednisolone) — no proven benefit for disc radiculopathy and increased infection risk.
- **Do NOT** order a full spine MRI for simple back pain without radicular signs — low yield and wastes resource.
- **Do NOT** prescribe opioids as first-line for chronic radicular pain — risk of dependence.
- **Do NOT** tell patient “it will go away in 2 weeks” — symptoms may persist 6–12 weeks; set realistic expectations.
- **Do NOT** perform a lumbar puncture in a patient with suspected disc herniation — risk of herniation worsening if LP is at the wrong level.
- **Do NOT** immobilise with a brace — no evidence; early mobilisation is better.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Disc Herniation | Lumbar Strain (mechanical back pain) | Lumbar Spinal Stenosis | Hip Osteoarthritis (referred pain) | Sacroiliac Joint Dysfunction |
|---------|----------------|--------------------------------------|------------------------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Pain distribution** | Radicular (dermatomal) leg pain; back pain often resolves | Non-radicular, dull central low back, no leg pain | Bilateral leg pain/aching with walking (pseudoclaudication), relieved by bending forward | Groin pain ± referred to knee; lateral thigh rare | Unilateral gluteal pain; can radiate to posterior thigh but not below knee |
| **Exacerbating factors** | Sitting, coughing, sneezing, lifting (increase intradiscal pressure) | Movement aggravates; but no specific root stretch | Walking or standing (extends spine → narrows canal) | Weight-bearing, internal rotation of hip | Standing on one leg, climbing stairs, unilateral loading |
| **Physical exam** | +SLR, +crossed SLR, reflex loss, dermatomal sensory loss, motor weakness | Normal SLR (may have mild low back pain but no leg pain); no reflex loss | Normal SLR; neurologic exam often normal at rest; may have broad-based gait | Hip flexion, internal rotation, abduction limited (FABER test +) | FABER test + (pain in SI region); >2/4 SI provocation tests positive |
| **Imaging** | MRI: disc protrusion with nerve compression | Normal MRI or minor degenerative changes | MRI: central canal stenosis (sagittal diameter <10 mm) | X-ray hip: joint space narrowing, osteophytes | X-ray SI joints normal; MRI STIR shows marrow edema (sacroliitis) |
| **Key differentiating feature** | **Dermatomal leg pain + corresponding reflex loss + positive SLR** | No radicular symptoms; negative SLR; resolves with simple analgesia | **Bilateral leg pain with walking improves on sitting/bending forward** | **Groin pain + limited hip internal rotation** | **Unilateral gluteal pain + SI provocation tests; no dermatomal pattern** |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**1. Cauda Equina Syndrome (CES)** – The must-not-miss complication.

- **How it presents:** Bilateral leg pain or numbness, saddle anaesthesia (perineum, buttocks, inner thighs), urinary retention (overflow incontinence), faecal incontinence, loss of anal tone, bilateral lower limb weakness (often flaccid).
- **Key signs:**  
  - Decreased or absent perianal sensation (S2–S4).  
  - Loss of anal wink reflex.  
  - Post-void residual volume >200 mL on bladder scan.  
  - GCS normal (unless spinal shock).
- **What to do:**  
  - **Immediate:** Urgent MRI (whole spine; may be from a higher level).  
  - **Concurrent:** Neurosurgery consult — do not wait for MRI results if clinical suspicion is high.  
  - **Timing:** Surgery within 24 hours of symptom onset significantly improves outcomes.  
  - **Pitfall:** Delayed diagnosis due to “the patient says they can still pass urine” (but may be in overflow incontinence). Always perform a bladder scan.

**2. Chronic radiculopathy / chronic pain:**

- **Presentation:** Persistent numbness, paraesthesia, or weakness beyond 3 months despite conservative care.  
- **Key signs:** Muscle atrophy (quadriceps L4, foot dorsiflexors L5), foot drop (L5), chronic ankle weakness.  
- **What to do:** EMG/NCS to quantify denervation; consider surgical decompression if significant weakness or pain.

**3. Progressive neurological deficit:**

- **How it presents:** Worsening motor loss (e.g., from slight foot drop to complete loss of dorsiflexion).  
- **Key signs:** Serial MRC scores dropping from 4+ to 3/5 or less over days/weeks.  
- **What to do:** Urgent surgical referral — motor loss may become irreversible after 6 months.

**4. Epidural abscess / discitis (rare but serious):**

- **How it presents:** Severe back pain with fever, night sweats, elevated CRP/ESR, leucocytosis; new or worsening neurological signs; may be post-procedural (e.g., after epidural injection).  
- **Key signs:** Fever, spinal tenderness on percussion, rigid paraspinal muscles.  
- **Imaging:** MRI with contrast shows high signal within disc space or epidural collection.  
- **Management:** Urgent microbiology consult; IV antibiotics (e.g., flucloxacillin 2 g q4h + rifampicin) and possible surgical drainage.

**Late-presentation patterns (the “untreated” stem):**

- A 60-year-old presents with bilateral foot drop and chronic urinary incontinence of 3 months’ duration, with MRI showing a large central disc herniation at L4-L5. This suggests missed CES — now likely permanent. She will need long-term catheterisation, mobility aids, and a multidisciplinary rehab team. Medicolegally, the delay is indefensible.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **Cauda Equina Syndrome: “Bowel, Bladder, Saddle, Motor, Shock”**  
   - **B**owel: loss of rectal tone / incontinence  
   - **B**ladder: retention / overflow incontinence  
   - **S**addle: perineal sensory loss (saddle anaesthesia)  
   - **M**otor: bilateral lower limb weakness (flaccid)  
   - **S**hock: spinal shock (hypotension, bradycardia) — rare but dramatic

2. **For lumbar nerve root levels (L4, L5, S1): “LPS” (Lay Patient Slow)**  
   - **L4**: Lateral thigh, knee jerk ↓, quadriceps weakness  
   - **P** (L5): Posterolateral shin/dorsum foot, no reflex, foot drop  
   - **S** (S1): Posterior calf/lateral foot, ankle jerk ↓, plantarflexion weakness

3. **Red flags for back pain (disc-specific): “MICTURITION”**  
   - **M**otor weakness (progressive)  
   - **I**ntractable pain (uncontrolled)  
   - **C**auda equina (bladder/bowel)  
   - **T**rauma (fracture risk)  
   - **U**rine retention  
   - **R**eflex loss (unilateral)  
   - **I**nfection (fever)  
   - **T**umour (night pain, weight loss)  
   - **I**mmunosuppression  
   - **O**ld age (>70)  
   - **N**eurological deficit (progressive)

**High-yield clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Crossed SLR = high specificity (90%) for disc herniation.** If raising the unaffected leg reproduces pain in the affected leg, you’re almost always dealing with a nerve root compression.
2. **Cough/sneeze test:** Patient says “when I cough, I go through the roof” — typical of disc herniation. The Valsalva increases intraspinal pressure, compressing the nerve root further.
3. **Sitting forward asymmetry:** Patient leans to one side in the waiting chair — that is an antalgic lean (opposite to the side of compression in lumbar disc). This is not “bad posture” — it’s a diagnostic clue.
4. **Foot drop + positive SLR always think L5 disc herniation** (L4-L5 disc). If a patient presents with foot drop, do a lumbar neuro exam — don’t jump to stroke or peroneal neuropathy without ruling out disc.
5. **Bilateral leg pain without back pain = high suspicion for central disc or spinal stenosis.** Don’t assume it’s a simple strain.
6. **Recurrent disc herniation after surgery (same level)** presents with identical symptoms after a symptom-free period of weeks to months. Suspect re-herniation; MRI with gadolinium may help differentiate scar vs disc (scar enhances).
7. **Cervical radiculopathy: Spurling’s test** — extend and rotate the neck to the symptomatic side and apply axial compression. Reproduction of arm pain is positive.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **Not doing a perianal sensory exam or rectal tone in anyone with bilateral leg symptoms** – This is the single biggest cause of missed CES.
- **Assuming a normal MRI of the lumbar spine rules out disc herniation** – Tiny lateral discs may be missed; correlate clinically.
- **Giving a muscle relaxant for “back spasm” without checking reflexes** – You may miss a S1 radiculopathy that will later show ankle weakness.
- **Ordering a plain X-ray
